retrieved_docs.title: Trauma Secondary Survey -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Rendering care to a trauma patient can be a challenging endeavor due to the potential for numerous injuries.  This part of evaluation should not be performed until the primary survey is completed. However, in patients with multi-system trauma, evaluation and management can often occur simultaneously. Therefore, secondary survey occurs both in series and in parallel with the primary survey. This evaluation ensures that all potentially life-threatening conditions have been identified and satisfactorily addressed. Not all injuries are immediately apparent in trauma patients. Occult injuries may be missed and delayed diagnosis can contribute to morbidity and mortality. The secondary survey is a rapid but thorough head-to-toe examination assessment to identify all potentially significant injuries. It is helpful to set the priorities for continued evaluation and management. It should be performed after the primary survey, and initial stabilization is complete. The purpose of the secondary survey is to obtain pertinent historical data about the patient and his or her injury, as well as to evaluate and treat all significant injuries not found during the primary survey by performing a systematic, complete examination. [1] [2] [3] [4] [5] Since trauma is a dynamic process, frequent reassessment is required in patients with trauma. If deterioration of clinical status is noted, primary survey should be repeated and evaluation of ABCs should be started. This will identify conditions, that require immediate intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma Secondary Survey -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The secondary survey is indicated in all trauma patients who have had their primary surveys completed.  The purpose of the secondary survey is to obtain a detailed history, perform a head-to-toe physical exam, reassess all vital signs, and obtain pertinent lab and imaging studies to identify injuries and metabolic abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma Secondary Survey -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The secondary survey is a systematic head-to-toe evaluation of trauma patients to identify injuries which were not recognized during the primary survey. [6] [7] [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma Care Principles -- Function -- The Secondary Survey
retrieved_docs.content: Once the primary survey is complete, the provider must gather more details about the events that led to the patient’s injuries and learn about their medical history to tailor the treatment plan. In a team environment, one member can complete the primary survey while another completes the secondary survey in parallel. Essentially, it is an expedited history and physical to make sure that nothing has been overlooked. This may be challenging in an unresponsive or altered patient. It is important to assess every organ system comprehensively. Again, providers can utilize any adjunct laboratory testing and imaging throughout the secondary survey. The elements to cover can be remembered using S.A.M.P.L.E.:

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma Secondary Survey -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: The secondary survey is a rapid but thorough head to toe examination assessment to identify potential injuries. It should be performed after the primary survey and the initial stabilization is complete. The purpose of the secondary survey is to obtain pertinent historical data about the patient and his or her injury, as well as to evaluate and treat injuries not found during the primary survey. It is helpful for prioritizing continued evaluation and management. This activity describes how a secondary survey is performed in a trauma patient and its clinical importance. This activity highlights the role of the interprofessional team in caring for trauma patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma Primary Survey -- Technique or Treatment -- Adjuncts to the Primary Survey:
retrieved_docs.content: A chest X-ray is obtained to evaluate for pneumothorax, hemothorax, or suspicion of an aortic injury.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma Primary Survey -- Technique or Treatment -- Adjuncts to the Primary Survey:
retrieved_docs.content: FAST Examination is the "Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma" and is performed to identify free fluid in the abdomen suggestive of intra-abdominal bleeding or pericardial tamponade.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma Secondary Survey -- Technique or Treatment -- Special Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Certain injuries may not be evident during the secondary survey unless specifically considered and evaluated. For example, injuries to the esophagus, diaphragm, and small bowel may remain unrecognized, even with good examination. A tertiary survey is helpful to identify injuries not recognized during the secondary survey. This should be performed in patients with multisystem trauma within the first 24 hours.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma Primary Survey -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the key systems evaluated in the primary survey of trauma patients. Explain how to perform the primary survey on trauma patients. Outline the steps involved in assessing airway patency, effective breathing, appropriate circulation, neurological dysfunction, and any other possible wounds. Summarize the importance of collaboration and communication amongst interprofessional team members to improve outcomes for trauma patients undergoing the primary survey. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma Primary Survey -- Technique or Treatment -- Adjuncts to the Primary Survey:
retrieved_docs.content: After the primary survey, the secondary survey is completed to ensure a comprehensive evaluation and management of the patient’s injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma Secondary Survey -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Articulate the steps involved in the secondary survey of a trauma patient. Summarize the potential complications of an incomplete secondary survey in a trauma patient. Explain the clinical significance of the secondary survey in a trauma patient. Explain the importance of clear communication and collaboration amongst interprofessional team members to improve outcomes for trauma patients undergoing the secondary survey. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma Primary Survey -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The common acronym for performing the primary trauma survey is ABCDE, each letter representing an area of focus. If any abnormality is identified in one of the areas of focus, it should be resolved before a practitioner progresses further through the algorithm. These steps are followed in the same order in every trauma resuscitation procedure to ensure that no critical or life-threatening injuries are overlooked. If a patient is noncooperative or combative and it interferes with conducting a proper primary trauma survey, then the patient should be sedated and intubated so that the exam may proceed. One caveat is that if a patient appears to be exsanguinating from a massive wound that can be addressed before starting the ABCDE algorithm, fortunately, the widespread adoption of the use of tourniquets in the field has limited the need to staunch massive bleeding in the trauma bay. [20] Below is each sequential area of focus for evaluation and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma Primary Survey -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: The primary survey is designed to assess and treat life-threatening injuries rapidly. The leading causes of death in trauma patients are airway obstruction, respiratory failure, hemorrhagic shock, and brain injury. Therefore, these are the areas targeted by the primary survey. Some injuries identified during a primary study include tension pneumothorax, open pneumothorax, airway obstruction, flail chest, massive internal or external hemorrhage, and cardiac tamponade. This activity reviews the steps, equipment, personnel, and techniques involved in performing the primary survey on trauma patients and also highlights the role of the interprofessional team in caring for trauma patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma Secondary Survey -- Technique or Treatment -- Physical Examination
retrieved_docs.content: The purpose of the secondary survey is to identify significant injuries. This involves a complete head-to-toe examination; examine all areas including front, back, sides, under and over. This examination involves a second review of the airway and lung examination. Throughout the evaluation, standard precautions for blood or fluid-borne infections should be observed.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma Primary Survey -- Technique or Treatment -- Adjuncts to the Primary Survey:
retrieved_docs.content: By the end of the primary survey, the trauma patient should have received a well-organized resuscitation, and any immediately life-threatening condition should have been identified and addressed. After completing the primary and secondary survey, there should be a decision on the patient's disposition: to obtain additional studies, proceed to the OR, take the patient to the ICU, or even progress to discharge if appropriate. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma Primary Survey -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Advanced trauma life-support care has been developed to standardize the evaluation and management of trauma patients since time is critical in trauma evaluation. The golden hour starts at the time of injury. This is the period during which timely and appropriate interventions can save the life of a patient that would otherwise die. A practitioner uses a primary survey to quickly assess, identify, and treat any life-threatening injuries if they exist.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2027
retrieved_docs.content: Open wounds (جرح مفتوح) are clinically associated with crushing injuries or internal injuries, indicating that such traumatic mechanisms often result in an open wound presentation. Within the category of open wounds, cut wounds (جرح قطع) represent a specific subtype, meaning that a cut wound is a particular form of open wound. Recognizing that crushing or internal injuries can lead to open wounds, and that cut wounds are a distinct subset of these, aids clinicians in diagnosing the nature of the trauma and selecting appropriate management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2082
retrieved_docs.content: Crushing injuries or internal injuries are associated with open wounds (جرح مفتوح), indicating that such traumatic forces often result in visible tissue disruption.

retrieved_docs.content: unspecified state of consciousness is associated with إصابة داخلية (internal injury), reinforcing the connection between severe intracranial bleeding and internal traumatic damage. Together, these relationships map a comprehensive hierarchy of general to specific injuries, illustrating how broad injury categories subdivide into distinct anatomical and pathological entities that are relevant for diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient care.

This structured relationship aids clinicians in linking acute injury diagnoses with their potential long‑term sequelae and facilitates comprehensive patient management across different injury types and body regions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 813
retrieved_docs.content: Internal injury of chest is recognized as a traumatic event and functions as a cause of subsequent medical complications, highlighting its role as an initiating injury in clinical assessments and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2463
retrieved_docs.content: Unspecified site injury is a broad injury category that encompasses more specific conditions such as intracranial injury, indicating that injuries without a clearly defined location may include trauma to the brain. This category is primarily derived from the concept of multiple injuries (إصابات متعددة), reflecting that when several injuries occur together or the exact site is not specified, they are often grouped under a general “unspecified site” label. Recognizing that unspecified site injury can represent both intracranial trauma and a collection of multiple injuries helps clinicians adopt a comprehensive assessment approach, ensuring that potential brain involvement is not overlooked and that the overall injury burden is appropriately addressed in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2591
retrieved_docs.content: The condition إصابة الرأس المغلقة (closed head injury) is clinically associated with تعاطي الكحول (alcohol consumption); alcohol use increases the likelihood of sustaining a closed head injury, and patients presenting with such injuries often have a history of recent alcohol intake. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians when assessing trauma patients, guiding both acute management and preventive counseling regarding alcohol use.

Overall, fracture (كسر) integrates late sequelae, specific anatomical fracture types, associated tissue damage, co‑occurring injuries, procedural interventions, and psychosocial classifications, providing a comprehensive framework for patient assessment and management.

Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in systematically assessing and managing the long‑term sequelae of diverse traumatic injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2105
retrieved_docs.content: Subarachnoid hemorrhage following injury with open intracranial wound, unspecified state of consciousness is associated with إصابة داخلية (internal injury), indicating that this form of hemorrhagic brain trauma often co‑occurs with internal injuries and should be evaluated together in clinical assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1781
retrieved_docs.content: Crushing injury or internal injury is associated with the medical condition referred to as إصابة (injury), indicating that such traumatic forces commonly result in injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2454
retrieved_docs.content: FACE INJURY TRAUMA and chest injury trauma are medical conditions that frequently co‑occur with حادثة سير (traffic accident). In patients involved in a traffic accident, clinicians should be alert to the high likelihood of both facial and chest injuries, as these trauma types commonly arise together in the context of such incidents. Recognizing this association aids in comprehensive assessment, timely diagnosis, and appropriate management of trauma patients following a traffic accident.

This integrated framework supports comprehensive assessment, classification, and management of patients presenting with complex traumatic or toxic presentations.
